coredata.dc:title: A numerical investigation into the application of thermosyphons for preventing the freezing of water meters, utilizing pure water and different nanofluids
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 The Author(s). Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Author(s). Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.dc:creator: Bahar, Amin
Ghafouri, Jafar
Zamzamian, Kamiar
coredata.dc:description: The study demonstrates that integrating thermosyphon systems with nanofluids significantly enhances the thermal efficiency of water meters in cold climates, effectively preventing freezing. A detailed numerical investigation revealed that nanofluids, such as alumina, copper oxide, and silver, outperform conventional fluids like pure water in heat transfer capabilities. The simulations showed that these nanofluids possess superior thermal conductivity and convective heat transfer properties, essential for maintaining temperatures above freezing in meter chambers. The research validated the numerical model against experimental data, confirming the effectiveness of the Volume of Fluid (VOF) approach for simulating two-phase fluid dynamics. Results indicated that increasing nanoparticle concentration directly affects heat flux and thermal performance, with higher concentrations leading to improved heat transfer rates and overall system efficiency.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nanofluid
Thermosyphon
Flow
Soil energy
VOF

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Temperature of soil at a depth corresponding to an ambient temperature of 255.75 K° (determined by data from the Tabriz meteorological synoptic station).
Hourly temperature recorded on the coldest day over the past decade, as derived from the data collected at the Tabriz meteorological synoptic station.
The geometry of the thermosyphon system.
Mesh Network developed for the study of results grid independency.
The study of results independency from the grid number Wall temperature of thermosiphon with work fluid Cu3 % nanofluid.
Comparison of pipe wall temperature with basic geometry for validation.
Mesh Network developed for the specified geometry.

Vapor and liquid fraction inside the thermosyphon system during the simulation time. a) Full length of thermosyphon b) detailed evaporator section of thermosyphon during simulation 50 percent of the evaporator section is initially filled with fluid.
Temperature contour of the thermosyphon system during the simulation time.

Comparison of Condenser wall Heat flux based on different particle concentration for Silver nanofluid According to Fig. 12, as concentration of particles increase the heat flux experience a slight increase.
Comparison of Condenser wall Heat flux based on different particle concentration for Copper Oxide nanofluid.
Comparison of Condenser wall Heat flux based on different particle concentration for aluminum oxide nanofluid.
Comparison of the effect of different nanofluids in same concentration on Condenser wall Heat flux.
Comparison of Thermosyphon wall Temperature based on particles concentration of copper oxide and pure water.
Comparison of Thermosyphon wall Temperature based on particles concentration of Silver and pure water.

head.ce:title: A numerical investigation into the application of thermosyphons for preventing the freezing of water meters, utilizing pure water and different nanofluids

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Mechanical Engineering, Tabriz Branch, Islamic Azad University, Tabriz, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Mechanical Engineering, Tabriz Branch, Islamic Azad University, Tabriz, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The study demonstrates that integrating thermosyphon systems with nanofluids significantly enhances the thermal efficiency of water meters in cold climates, effectively preventing freezing. A detailed numerical investigation revealed that nanofluids, such as alumina, copper oxide, and silver, outperform conventional fluids like pure water in heat transfer capabilities. The simulations showed that these nanofluids possess superior thermal conductivity and convective heat transfer properties, essential for maintaining temperatures above freezing in meter chambers. The research validated the numerical model against experimental data, confirming the effectiveness of the Volume of Fluid (VOF) approach for simulating two-phase fluid dynamics. Results indicated that increasing nanoparticle concentration directly affects heat flux and thermal performance, with higher concentrations leading to improved heat transfer rates and overall system efficiency.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The data that has been used is confidential.

ce:section.ce:para: Annually, the occurrence of damage and malfunction of water meters in frigid regions results in significant financial burdens for water and sewage companies, as well as their customers. Tabriz, a major city in Iran characterized by its cold climate, exemplifies this issue. The Tabriz synoptic weather station has recorded a minimum temperature of −17.4 °Celsius over the past decade. Consequently, in the present study a solution to mitigate this challenge by incorporating heat pipes within the meter housing has been explored. Recent advancements have identified heat pipes as a highly efficient method for the storage, retrieval, and transfer of thermal energy from one location to another without the need for additional energy input.

The concept of the heat pipe was initially introduced by Gaugler [1] in 1942, followed by Grover's theoretical and experimental investigations [2] in 1963. Heat pipes have found extensive application across various engineering fields, including solar energy systems [3], electronics [4], heat exchangers, and the food industry [5], as well as in space technology [6]. Their straightforward design, cost efficiency, reliability, and exceptional durability render them highly appealing for numerous industrial uses.

A heat pipe functions as a specialized thermosyphon system, incorporating a designated quantity of working fluid for the purpose of heat transfer. Within a sealed cylinder, the working fluid vaporizes in the evaporator section due to the heat it absorbs. This vapor then ascends through the adiabatic section to the condenser, where it releases heat and condenses back into a liquid. Subsequently, driven by gravitational forces, the liquid returns to the evaporator. The internal phase change cycle serves as the fundamental mechanism for heat transfer [7–9].

The choice of working fluid is crucial for the operational efficiency of a thermosyphon. Consequently, numerous researchers have conducted both numerical and experimental investigations into the application of nanofluids as the working fluid in heat pipes. The pioneering study on this subject was carried out by Chien et al. [10]. Nanofluids consist of a base fluid combined with nanoparticles. Extensive research has been undertaken to explore the performance characteristics of thermosyphons and heat pipes utilizing nanofluids, revealing that these fluids exhibit significantly enhanced thermal conductivity and convective heat transfer coefficients compared to conventional fluids such as water and oils [11–17].

Sureshkumar et al. [18], have investigated the heat transfer characteristics in heat pipes containing nanofluid. Their study provides a comprehensive examination of the research findings related to the heat transfer properties of heat pipes utilizing nanofluids as the working fluids. Keblinski et al. [19] conducted a comprehensive study on the application and analysis of various nanofluids in the context of heat transfer. Buschmann [20] conducted a comprehensive review and analysis of contemporary experimental studies and modeling efforts related to thermosyphons and heat pipes that utilize nanofluids.

Shafahi et al. [21] conducted a study on the thermal efficiency of cylindrical heat pipes utilizing various nanofluids. Their research employed a two-dimensional computational model to analyze the thermal performance of these heat pipes, incorporating three distinct types of nanoparticles: alumina, copper oxide, and titanium oxide within the nanofluid.

Cheng et al. [22] undertook a comprehensive investigation into the flow characteristics of nanofluids, focusing on parameters such as surface tension, thermal conductivity of the liquid, viscosity, and density. Their research also delves into the specific impacts these properties have on the behavior of two-phase flow and the heat transfer capabilities of nanofluids within heat pipes.

Zhou and Vafai [23] conducted an investigation into the impact of nanofluids on cylindrical heat pipes by employing a detailed analytical model. Their study focused on a specific range of concentrations and varying heat input levels, utilizing different types of nanoparticles. The research examined the performance of alumina, copper oxide, and titanium oxide suspended in a water-based fluid, leading to the determination of temperature profiles, thermal resistance, and the maximum heat transfer capabilities of the heat pipes.

Kang et al. [24] conducted an experimental study to examine the thermal properties of a heat pipe utilizing silver nanofluid. In their study, DI-water diluted with 10 nm and 35 nm silver particles, inside a 211×217 µm deep grooved circular heat pipe was experimentally tested. The results of the performance test were as follows; firstly, with greater silver nano-particles dispersed in working fluid, the increase in heat pipe wall temperature was smaller than that for a pure water filled heat pipe under various heat loads. Secondly, comparing two nano-particle sizes with the thermal resistance value using DI-water, the maximum reduction was 50 % (10 nm) and 80 % (35 nm), respectively. Therefore, the thermal resistance of grooved heat pipe appears to be a function of the size of the nano-particles.

The research conducted by Parametthanuwat et al. [25] examined the heat transfer efficiency of a thermosyphon utilizing nanofluid, demonstrating that the incorporation of nanofluid enhances the efficiency of the thermosyphon system. The silver nanofluid with a concentration of 1 w/v% oleic acid demonstrated superior performance across all experimental trials. Additionally, the optimal operating conditions identified were an 80 °C temperature, a 50 % filling ratio, and a volumetric flow rate of 5 l/min, which resulted in the highest heat flux from the thermoelectric power generation system (TPEC). Moreover, the maximum effectiveness was also observed with the silver nanofluid containing 1 w/v% oleic acid under the same temperature of 80 °C, a filling ratio of 50 %, and a volumetric flow rate of 1 l/min. Some researchers [26–30] have reported the most optimal concentrations of nanofluids for use in heat pipes in their studies.

Kamyar et al. [31] conducted an investigation into the thermal efficiency of a two-phase thermosyphon utilizing water-based nanofluids composed of alumina and titanium silicon oxide. The preparation of these nanofluids involved varying volume concentrations and heat loads of evaporator. Their findings indicate that the application of both nanofluids significantly enhanced the performance of the heat pipe, achieving a reduction in thermal resistance of 65 % for alumina nanofluid at a volume percentage of 0.05 %, and 57 % for titanium silicon oxide nanofluid at a volume percentage of 0.075 %.

Kalantarpour et al. [32] Enhancing The role of self-rewetting nanofluids, and filling ratios for improved performance of heat transfer in thermosyphons the investigation includes the impact of different working fluids, filling ratios, and heat power on the thermal performance of the system. in their work they use VOF technique to simulating a closed, flat-shaped thermosyphon to Optimizing the thermal performance of the thermosyphon heat pipe Results have shown that self-rewetting nanofluids outperform traditional working fluids, demonstrating enhanced heat transfer capabilities and a reduced risk of dry-out.

Ghorabaee., H. et al. [33] in a comprehensive review article studied the use of nanofluids in thermosyphon heat pipe they study the previous works to understand the dominant mechanisms of heat transfer when nanofluids are used in thermosyphon heat pipe (THP). The influence of heat input, type and concentration of nanoparticles on the thermal behavior of THPs are investigated. In general, nanofluids significantly improve the thermal characteristics of THP by decreasing the thermal resistance and increase the thermal efficiency of THP. The thermal performance of THP strongly depends on operating variables, especially the type of nanoparticles, their concentration and addition of surfactant and heat inputs. Further, the thermal performance of THP at various heat input is remarkably related to the type of nanofluid. This study also presents a review of the applications of the nanofluid-based heat pipes in energy systems. their results showed that nanofluids increase thermal efficiency and decrease thermal resistance of thermosyphon HP. Also, there is an optimum concentration for the nanofluids that THP has the best thermal performance using them.

Ghorabaee., H. et al. [34] investigate the Effect of Nanofluid and Surfactant on Thermosyphon Heat Pipe Performance. They are experimentally with three type of fluid, namely, distilled water, nanofluid (titanium dioxide), and a mixture of nanofluid and surfactant investigated the performance of THP. results indicated that with increasing the concentration of nanofluid, thermal efficiency increases and thermal resistance of the thermosyphon decreases. According to the results, mixing the nanofluid with the surfactant will decrease the evaporator wall temperature and the thermal resistance, while it increases the thermal efficiency of THP. Comparison between two nanofluids and a conventional fluid in a THP shows that the best pipe inclination angles are 60° for water and 90° for nanofluids. Adding the proper amount of surfactant increases THP's thermal efficiency by 20 %. The best thermal performance of THP achieved at the input power of 200 W for all the working fluids .

Alizadeh et al. [8] conducted a comprehensive study utilizing both a numerical two-dimensional model and an experimental framework to examine the influence of input heat and the filling ratios of the working fluid on thermosyphon efficiency. To validate their numerical model, they compared its outcomes with experimental data. Their investigation encompassed three distinct input heat fluxes of 700, 500, and 350 Watt, alongside three filling ratios of 0.3, 0.5, and 0.8. The results revealed that an increase in input heat flux from 350 to 500 W led to an enhancement in thermosyphon efficiency. Furthermore, they identified that optimal efficiency was achieved at a filling ratio of 0.5. The researchers employed the Volume of Fluid (VOF) numerical method to simulate phase interactions, and the predicted temperature distribution aligned well with results obtained from computational fluid dynamics, corroborating the experimental findings. They concluded that computational fluid dynamics serves as an effective tool for modeling and elucidating complex flow dynamics and heat transfer processes within the thermosyphon.

Computational fluid dynamics (CFD) serves as an essential method for the simulation of fluid behavior and thermal management in various industrial contexts. When modeling the efficacy of heat pipes through CFD, it is imperative to employ multiphase models to accurately represent boiling and condensation processes. A significant challenge in this modeling approach is the accurate determination of the distribution of liquid and gas phases. The Volume of Fluid (VOF) model is one of the most prevalent methodologies employed to address such challenges. This model functions as a surface tracking technique specifically designed for scenarios involving two or more immiscible fluids with distinct boundaries. The VOF model operates by solving a series of momentum equations that are applicable to the fluids, while also accounting for the volume fraction of each fluid within individual computational cells. In cases where mass transfer between phases is present, it becomes necessary to incorporate additional parameters into the mass conservation equations for both phases. Likewise, the latent heat of vaporization must be integrated into the energy equation [35].

The researches outlined posits that a temperature of 274.15 Kelvin shall be maintained within the meter chamber to avert freezing, while this temperature is also considered as a representative of the surrounding environment and the external surface of the heat pipe condenser.

For the purpose of the current study, a two-dimensional numerical model is employed to assess the efficiency of a heat pipe utilizing water and various nanofluids as the working fluid, aimed at elevating the temperature within the water meter chamber by facilitating heat transfer from a depth of one meter in the soil Fig. 1 .

Notably, data from the Synoptic weather station in Tabriz indicates that the temperature of the soil at one meter depth on the coldest day over the past decade was recorded at 7.4 °Celsius, suggesting a significant potential for heat transfer from deeper soil layers to the meter chamber through a Thermosyphon mechanism, thereby preventing freezing. Simulations will be conducted for different nanofluids, including copper oxide, alumina, and silver at varying concentrations and the optimal configuration will be identified. Furthermore, the heat transfer capabilities of a thermosyphon system will be analyzed, which will involve the integration of multiple thermosyphons within the chamber, as illustrated in Fig. 2 .

The novelty of this paper lies in several key aspects:
•
Integration of Nanofluids in Thermosyphon Systems: The study uniquely focuses on the application of nanofluids, such as alumina, copper oxide, and silver, as working fluids in thermosyphon systems specifically designed to prevent the freezing of water meters. This approach leverages the enhanced thermal properties of nanofluids to improve heat transfer efficiency.
•
Numerical Modeling and Validation: The paper employs a sophisticated two-dimensional numerical model using the Volume of Fluid (VOF) method to analyze two-phase fluid dynamics. This modeling technique is validated against experimental data, providing a reliable framework for predicting the performance of thermosyphons in real-world scenarios.
•
Focus on Cold Climate Applications: By addressing the specific challenges faced by water meters in cold regions, particularly in Tabriz, Iran, the research directly contributes to solving a practical problem that has significant economic implications for water and sewage companies.
•
Comprehensive Analysis of Heat Transfer Mechanisms: The study investigates various parameters, including nanoparticle concentration and soil temperature, to assess their impact on the thermal performance of the thermosyphon system. This comprehensive analysis contributes to a deeper understanding of the factors influencing heat transfer in such applications.
•
Potential for Practical Implementation: The findings suggest practical applications for enhancing the reliability of water supply systems in freezing conditions, making the research relevant for engineers and policymakers in regions susceptible to cold weather.
Overall, this paper presents innovative solutions and insights into the use of advanced materials and modeling techniques to tackle a critical issue in thermal management for water infrastructure.

The Volume of Fluid (VOF) model, introduced by Hirt and Nichols [36], is utilized for the analysis of free surface dynamics in two-phase fluids. The volume fraction of each phase must be incorporated into the calculations. In the VOF model, the sum of the volume fractions of all phases within any control volume equals one. In Computational Fluid Dynamics (CFD) modeling, the Navier-Stokes equations are solved simultaneously. The governing equations for the VOF model are expressed as follows:

By applying the physical principle of conservation of mass for the fluid, the continuity equation is obtained as follows:
(1)

S M

In Eq. (1), ρ is density,
u j
represents velocity and
S M
is mass term which is used for mass transfer rate in vaporization and condensation processes.

In the equations of continuity, momentum and energy, the density and viscosity of the fluid depend on the volume fraction of each phase, which are calculated from the following equations:
(2)

The forces influencing the fluid include gravitational, pressure, friction, and surface tension forces. To account for the impact of surface tension at the interface between two phases, the continuous surface force model proposed by Brackbill et al. [37] is utilized. It was recommended that the term (
S

) be incorporated into the momentum equation. Taking these forces into consideration, the momentum equation for the Volume of Fluid (VOF) model can be expressed as follows:

The momentum equation is addressed throughout the computational domain, and the derived velocity is distributed among the various phases.

The energy equation for the VOF model is written as follows:
(5)

In Eq. (5), E is the total energy per mass and
S E
is source term of energy which is used for heat transfer calculation during vaporization and condensation processes. Heat transfer coefficient can be calculated via Eq. (6).
(6)

1. Furthermore, the VOF model considers temperature (T) and energy (E) as averaged variables of mass, as illustrated in the subsequent equation.
(7)

In Eq. (7), the energy per unit mass for each phase and determined by the specific heat and temperature of that phase. The volume fraction delineates the relationship between the two phases. Consequently, by applying the mass balance along the interface of the phases, the equation representing the conservation of the volume fraction can be articulated as follows:
(8)
∂ α

+ u ∇ α = 0

The Volume of Fluid (VOF) model is predicated on the principle that each cell within the computational domain is filled by a single phase or a mixture of the two phases. Specifically, when the volume fraction is designated as liquid and the volume fraction as vapor, three distinct conditions may arise.

s t a t e

When the temperature of the liquid phase surpasses the saturation temperature, a phase transition occurs, resulting in the conversion of mass from the liquid state to the vapor state, accompanied by the absorption of energy.

(9)

Same can be said for dynamic viscosity and heat conduction coefficient [40,41].

As stated earlier, in the present study several nanofluid will be studied and the heat transfer capability of each of these nanofluids will be simulated and compared. The specification of the nanofluid considered in this study is according to the work of Yu et al. [41] which is presented in Table 1 .

Based on the soil temperature data collected during this extreme cold event, a copper heat pipe, as illustrated in Fig. 5 has been designed and details of this design has been provided in Table 2 . The design considered a filling ratio of 50 % of the evaporator volume with working fluids, specifically pure water and a mixture of water with alumina nanofluid. This study aims to evaluate the performance of these fluids and the influence of the nanofluid on the thermosyphon's heat transfer efficiency.

Given the complexities associated with modeling two-phase fluid dynamics within the thermosyphon, we will employ Ansys Fluent Version 19, utilizing the VOF multiphase model for our simulations. The primary objective of this research is to ensure that the temperature within the meter chamber remains at least 1 °C above freezing Temperature, thereby assessing the thermosyphon's capability to transfer heat from the soil depths to maintain this critical temperature. Subsequently, this paper will analyze the impact of employing alumina nanofluid in conjunction with water, as opposed to using pure water alone, on the enhancement of heat transfer performance by the thermosyphon.

This study examines the heat transfer between the soil and a meter chamber, which has dimensions that include a depth of 50 cm. Two thermal pipes, each measuring 60 cm, are installed, following the specifications outlined in Table 2. The analysis utilizes the temperature gradient between the soil's depth and the meter chamber. The investigation operates under the premise that the thermosyphon extracts heat from both the lower section and the evaporator wall, while being fully insulated in the adiabatic region, where the heat flux is assumed to be negligible. In the condenser section, which encompasses the upper 10 cm of the thermosyphon situated within the meter chamber, heat transfer processes are addressed.

As indicated in Fig. 5, the temperature at the bottom wall of the thermosyphon is recorded at 280.55 K, while the evaporator wall temperature is estimated at 279.55 K, reflecting the average temperature of the soil at depths ranging from 100 to 72 cm. The wall temperature of the condenser section is measured at 265.2 K, based on a model of the meter chamber's internal temperature without the thermosyphon. The saturation pressure within the thermosyphon is established at 835 Pascal, corresponding to a saturation temperature of 277.35 K. Additionally, the analysis assumes a no-slip condition and a gravitational acceleration of 9.81 m/s² for the internal walls.

In this research, for numerical simulation of solving domain using finite volume method, the number of organized grid number was 11,937, 26,697, 67,850 and 131,571. To avoid results dependency to the chosen grid number, the parameters of temperature of the wall were compared. According to the changes of mentioned parameters, the grid number of 67,850 was chosen for this simulation. The boundary layer technique was used to mesh near-wall regions of the thermosyphon. Five rows of mesh with initial size of 0.1 mm and growth factor of 1.2 was used. To increase the accuracy of solving, SIMPLEC algorithm was used for discretizing the governing equations. The maximum amount of results convergence of this simulation is
10
− 5
.

To ensure validation, the outcomes derived from the numerical modeling were juxtaposed with the experimental findings reported by Alizadeh et al. [8]. Initially, the geometry pertinent to this study was constructed in the Ansys Design Modular software, as outlined in Table 2. Subsequently, the geometry was meshed using Ansys Meshing software. The heat pipe's geometry was discretized following the methodology of Alizadeh et al. [8], resulting in a mesh comprising 47,124 nodes. This mesh included five rows of boundary layers on each wall, with an initial thickness of 0.1 mm and a growth factor of 1.2 Fig. 6 , Fig. 7.

The simulation was conducted using Ansys Fluent 19, employing the VOF multiphase model and the SIMPLE algorithm, designating the liquid phase as primary and the vapor phase as secondary, with a time step of 0.001 s over 10,000 iterations, under input and output flux conditions of 29,318 W per square meter on evaporator wall. Fig. 8 illustrates the temperature distribution on the heat pipe wall, derived from the geometrical parameters specified in Table-3, and compares it with the results obtained by Alizadeh. The consistency between their findings and those of the current study substantiates the validity of the employed solution methodology.

The results of vapor fraction and temperature of the fluid along the system are presented in form of contours in Figs. 10 and 11
for simulation the time domain.

As can be seen in Figs. 10 and 11, the heat from the soil will vaporize the fluid and this heat is transferred to the condenser section of the system via natural convection and is cooled to the ambient temperature which in turn will condense the fluid and the condensed fluid will fall to the bottom of the system and heated again. Fig. 10 illustrate the heat flux rate for silver imbued nanofluid. Impact of different concentration of nano particles on heat flux has been studied.

Fig. 13 illustrate the heat flux rate for copper oxide nanofluid. Impact of different concentration of nano particles on heat flux has been studied. As expected, increasing the particles concentrations will cause an increase in heat flux. Same analysis has been made for Aluminum oxide nanofluid for different concentrations. The results show an uptrend in flux with concentration of nanofluid increase.
In order to better Al2O3 the heat transfer capability of nano fluids with different concentrations in Fig. 14 has been provided.
Temperature along the length of the system for different nanofluid has been plotted and illustrated in Fig. 15 .
Fig. 16 , Fig. 17

The study provides evidence that the integration of thermosyphon systems with nanofluids can significantly enhance the thermal efficiency of water meters in cold climates, effectively preventing freezing. Through a detailed numerical investigation, it was established that the use of nanofluids, specifically alumina, copper oxide, and silver, improves heat transfer capabilities compared to conventional fluids like pure water. The simulations demonstrated that these nanofluids exhibit superior thermal conductivity and convective heat transfer properties, which are crucial for maintaining temperatures above freezing within the meter chamber. The research also validated the numerical model against experimental data, confirming the reliability of the Volume of Fluid (VOF) approach in simulating two-phase fluid dynamics. The results indicated that varying the concentration of nanoparticles directly influences the heat flux and thermal performance of the thermosyphon system. Notably, higher concentrations of nanofluids resulted in increased heat transfer rates, thereby enhancing the overall efficiency of the system. Moreover, the findings underscore the importance of selecting appropriate working fluids and optimizing their concentrations to maximize the effectiveness of heat pipes in real-world applications. The ability to extract heat from deeper soil layers through thermosyphon mechanisms presents a viable solution to mitigate the financial burdens associated with water meter damage in freezing conditions.

In conclusion, this study not only advances the understanding of thermosyphon systems and nanofluids but also paves the way for practical applications in cold regions. The implementation of these technologies can lead to improved reliability and cost-effectiveness for water and sewage companies, ultimately benefiting consumers by reducing service interruptions and associated costs. Further research could explore the long-term performance and durability of these systems under varying environmental conditions, ensuring their feasibility for widespread adoption.

Amin Bahar: Writing – review & editing, Methodology, Investigation, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Jafar Ghafouri: Visualization, Validation, Supervision, Software, Resources, Project administration. Kamiar Zamzamian: Writing – original draft, Visualization, Supervision, Project administration, Formal analysis, Data curation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Gaugler, R.S., Heat Transfer Device, G.M. Corporation, Editor. 1942: USA.
Grover, G.M., Evaporation-Condensation Heat Transfer Device, U.S.A.E. Commission, Editor. 1963: USA.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Theoretical and experimental investigation of heat pipe solar collector

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Azad, E., Theoretical and experimental investigation of heat pipe solar collector. Exp. Therm. Fluid. Sci., 2008. 32 (8): p. 1666–1672.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Heat pipe heat exchanger for heat recovery in air conditioning

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Abd El-Baky M.A. and M.M. Mohamed, Heat pipe heat exchanger for heat recovery in air conditioning. Appl. Therm. Eng., 2007. 27 (4): p. 795–801.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Thermal applicability of two-phase thermosyphons in cooking chambers––experimental and theoretical analysis

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Da Silva, A. and M. Mantelli, Thermal applicability of two-phase thermosyphons in cooking chambers––experimental and theoretical analysis. Appl. Therm. Eng., 2004. 24 (5–6): p. 717–733.
